title: React Testing & QA Specialist Mock Test
description: Foundational and intermediate assessment for QA engineers and developers focusing on Vitest/Jest, React Testing Library, Cypress, and Playwright.

questions.text: What is the primary philosophy of React Testing Library (RTL)?
options: To test the internal state and methods of a component
To test components in a way that resembles how a user interacts with them
To replace the need for Jest or Vitest
questions.explanation: RTL encourages testing behavior (what the user sees/does) rather than implementation details (state/props), making tests more resilient to refactoring.

questions.text: Which RTL query should be your first choice for finding an input field according to best practices?
questions.explanation: getByRole (e.g., role='textbox') is preferred because it mimics how assistive technology perceives the page, promoting accessibility.

questions.text: What is the difference between `queryBy...` and `getBy...` in React Testing Library?
options: queryBy returns a promise, getBy is synchronous
getBy throws an error if the element is not found; queryBy returns null
getBy is only used for buttons
questions.explanation: Use getBy when you expect the element to exist. Use queryBy when you want to assert that an element is NOT in the document.

questions.text: How do you handle testing a component that performs an asynchronous API call on mount?
options: Use `findBy...` queries which return a promise and retry until the element appears
Wrap the render in a setTimeout
questions.explanation: findBy queries are a combination of getBy and waitFor. They are the standard way to handle elements that appear asynchronously.

questions.text: In Jest/Vitest, what is a 'Mock' function used for?
options: To simulate a function and track calls, arguments, and return values
To bypass the need for a browser
questions.explanation: Mocks (vi.fn() or jest.fn()) allow you to isolate the component by simulating external dependencies like API services or child component callbacks.

questions.text: Which tool is specifically designed for 'End-to-End' (E2E) testing of a React application in a real browser?
questions.explanation: Playwright (and Cypress) are E2E tools that run tests against a fully deployed app in actual browser engines.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `act()` utility in React testing?
options: To simulate a user acting on a button
To ensure all updates (state, effects) are processed and applied to the DOM before making assertions
To measure the performance of an action
questions.explanation: act() ensures that the 'unit' of work is finished. RTL wraps most calls in act() automatically, but manual usage is sometimes needed for custom hooks or complex async logic.

options: Re-running existing tests to ensure new changes haven't broken current functionality
Testing the app on older versions of Windows
questions.explanation: Regressions are bugs in previously working features. Automated suites are primarily designed to catch these.

questions.text: When using `fireEvent` vs `user-event` library, why is `user-event` generally preferred?
options: It more accurately dispatches multiple events (like mouseover, mousedown, mouseup) to better simulate real interaction
questions.explanation: user-event is a higher-level abstraction that simulates full interactions (like typing a string instead of just a change event), leading to more realistic tests.

questions.text: What is the 'Testing Pyramid' recommendation for a React app?
options: Mostly E2E tests, few unit tests
Equal amounts of all test types
Mostly Unit and Integration tests, with fewer E2E tests
questions.explanation: Unit and Integration tests are faster and cheaper to run. E2E tests are powerful but slow and brittle, so they should cover critical paths only.
